mechanism: A latent construct (trait inspiration) is operationalized through survey items. Correlations and factor structure validate the measure's internal consistency. Nomological network relationships are established by computing associations between the latent trait and various external outcomes (patents held, daily inspiration experiences, behavioral/emotional consequences). Validity is assessed through multiple correlation matrices across studies, comparing the strength and pattern of relationships in new contexts.

Participants view video stimuli depicting human actions. A coding system categorizes each observed action hierarchically (low-level motor details vs. high-level intentional/goal effects). Separately, attributions of mental states are assessed through ratings or categorical judgments. Covariation between action identification level and mind attribution scores is computed across participants and experimental conditions via correlation and contingency analysis.

Participants make a binary decision. Brain imaging (fMRI) records left frontal cortical activation during post-decision periods. Neurofeedback-based intervention modulates activation in real time, or an independent manipulation (action-oriented mindset) is induced. The magnitude of post-decisional attitude change (spreading of alternatives) is measured. The relationship between the intervention, cortical activation level, and attitude change magnitude is quantified via regression or ANOVA.
domain: Social and cognitive neuroscience, decision making

mechanism: A theoretical model posits that victims of conflict face specific resource threats (status/power loss) while perpetrators face different threats (moral image damage). An intervention (social exchange interaction) is designed to address these threats selectively. Multiple outcome measures assess willingness to reconcile before and after intervention. Cross-group comparisons and correlations between threat salience, need satisfaction, and reconciliation willingness are computed.

Participants are experimentally assigned to imagine scenarios varying temporal constraint (final vs. repeat visits to a meaningful location). Emotional experience is reported on discrete emotion scales (happiness, sadness, and joint occurrence). In parallel, age or time horizon is measured. The frequency and intensity of mixed emotional states are computed for each condition. Correlations between temporal boundary salience, time horizon perception, and mixed emotion prevalence are quantified.

Implicit and explicit attitude measures are administered varying in their structural demands (task format, response modality, information integration requirements). For each pair of tests, the correlation coefficient is computed. Tests are systematically made more or less similar in structure. The hypothesis is that structural similarity (measured as task format overlap or cognitive demand alignment) predicts the implicit-explicit correlation magnitude.

Reaction time sequences from a computerized task are collected per participant. Fourier spectral analysis decomposes the temporal series into frequency components. The amplitude spectrum is examined; 1/f noise is identified when log-amplitude decreases linearly with log-frequency. The magnitude of this spectral slope is quantified as a noise parameter. Participants' effort level (measured or manipulated) is correlated with the slope parameter, with the hypothesis that increased effort reduces spectral slope magnitude.

Participants are exposed to either threat (stereotype-activation) or control conditions prior to an interracial interaction. Physical proximity or distance to a conversation partner is measured during the interaction. Separately, implicit and explicit prejudice measures are obtained, and stereotype activation is assessed via priming tasks. Mediation and correlation analyses test whether threat perception predicts distance independently of explicit/implicit prejudice, and whether stereotype activation mediates this link.

Participants report ideal romantic partner preferences on multiple dimensions (attractiveness, earning potential). In a separate speed-dating study, participants meet real potential partners; romantic interest is measured. Simultaneously, objective partner attributes (measured or coded attractiveness, earnings) are recorded. Correlations between stated ideals and actual choices are computed. Separately, correlations between actual interest and partner attributes are computed. Sex differences in both patterns are tested via group-stratified correlation and regression.

Participants are primed with either high or low importance values before reading a persuasive message. Message comprehension or elaboration is measured (via thought-listing, reading time, or recall). Later, persuasion resistance is tested by introducing counterarguments and measuring attitude change. Value importance level is related to processing depth via correlation. Processing depth is related to resistance magnitude. Mediation analysis tests whether processing depth mediates the value-importance-to-resistance pathway.

Participants undergo either a self-regulation depletion manipulation or control. They then evaluate sets of information varying in stance-consistency. Information preference is measured as the fraction of consistent information selected or the amount of attention allocated to consistent vs. inconsistent items. Commitment to one's position is measured independently. ANOVA compares preference profiles between depletion and control groups. Mediation analysis tests whether position commitment explains the depletion effect on information preference.
domain: Social psychology, decision making and motivation

mechanism: Participants make a decision and/or plan implementation (vs. deliberation or control). Attitude properties toward various target objects are then measured: extremity (rating scale), ambivalence (joint positive and negative evaluation), and accessibility (response latency to attitude probe). These attitude measures are compared across mind-set conditions via ANOVA. Behavior is measured at follow-up and correlated with initial attitude. Mediation testing examines whether focus on supportive information explains the mind-set effect on attitude properties.
domain: Social psychology, attitudes and decision implementation

mechanism: Risk (relationship vulnerability cues) and self-esteem are manipulated or measured. Participants' goal activation (connectedness vs. self-protection) is measured implicitly or explicitly. Executive control strength is either measured or manipulated. Outcome measures include avoidance/approach behavior and relational outcomes. Moderated mediation analyses test whether risk activates competing goals, executive control resolves the conflict conditionally on self-esteem, and goal activation predicts behavior.

Participants are assessed for whether they have committed offenses similar to a described transgression (via self-report or scenario manipulation). Forgiveness judgment toward a transgressor is measured on a scale. Three potential mediators are measured: perceived severity of the transgressor's offense, empathic understanding of the transgressor, and perceived similarity to the transgressor. Mediation analysis tests whether personal capability for the offense predicts forgiveness through these three pathways, with sex as a moderator.
domain: Social psychology, forgiveness and interpersonal relations

mechanism: Video recordings or still photographs show a target displaying the pride facial expression (head tilt, chest expansion, arm positioning). Participants from different cultural groups view the stimuli and classify or rate the displayed emotion. The frequency of correct classification is tabulated. Cross-cultural and within-culture agreement rates are compared (either via accuracy percentages or kappa statistics). Stimuli are varied systematically (ethnicity, gender, target) to test generalization of recognition.

Priming (happy or sad concepts) is crossed factorially with mood induction (happy or sad feelings), motor action (approach or avoidance), or facial expression (happy or sad). Coherence between primed concepts and induced states is operationally defined. Cognitive performance (recall of a story) is measured after each condition. ANOVA tests whether coherence vs. incoherence between conceptual and experiential affective information predicts performance.

Processing style (global vs. local) is induced via perceptual priming or time perspective manipulation. A subsequent social judgment task involves target information that could be assimilated (inclusion) or contrasted (exclusion) against a comparison/context category. Judgment magnitude is measured. ANOVA or regression tests whether processing style (global/local) predicts judgment direction (assimilation/contrast). Hemisphere activation (fMRI or EEG) and knowledge accessibility are measured as potential mediators.

In an ultimatum bargaining paradigm, a player either communicates anger or happiness. The opponent's behavior (acceptance/rejection, offer level) and final outcomes are recorded. The structural context is varied (opportunity to deceive, consequence severity). A theoretical model predicts when emotion communication succeeds (opponent perceives higher limits and yields) vs. backfires (opponent punishes or mistrusts). Outcome comparisons across emotions and structural conditions test these predictions.

Workplace or institutional setting cues (minority representation, diversity philosophy) are presented to participants from a stigmatized group. Perceived social identity contingencies (judgments, stereotypes, opportunities tied to identity) and trust in the setting are measured. Experimental conditions manipulate cue combinations. Mediation analysis tests whether identity contingency perceptions explain the effect of environmental cues on trust.
domain: Organizational and social psychology, diversity and belonging

mechanism: A participant's own felt communal responsiveness toward a partner (measured or manipulated) is measured. Independently, perceptions of the partner's communal responsiveness are assessed. Relationship outcomes (attraction, warmth, self-disclosure, relationship satisfaction) are measured cross-sectionally and longitudinally. Correlation and regression analyses test whether own responsiveness predicts perceived partner responsiveness, and whether perceived responsiveness mediates the link between own responsiveness and relationship outcomes.
domain: Social psychology, relationships and emotional bonds
